message.text: Email code login verification endpoint lacks rate limiting on code attempts. The EmailCodeLoginApi.post() method checks the code against token_data but does not limit the number of incorrect attempts, allowing brute-force of short verification codes.

OAuth state parameter is repurposed as invite_token instead of being used for CSRF protection. In OAuthCallback.get(), the 'state' query parameter is directly assigned to invite_token without any CSRF validation, defeating the purpose of the OAuth state parameter and enabling cross-site request forgery attacks on the OAuth flow.

Registration endpoint returns access/refresh token pair in the JSON response body instead of HTTP-only secure cookies. Unlike LoginApi and EmailCodeLoginApi which use set_access_token_to_cookie(), EmailRegisterResetApi returns token_pair.model_dump() directly, exposing tokens to JavaScript and XSS attacks.

Password reset endpoint returns the reset token in the HTTP response body. ResetPasswordSendEmailApi.post() returns {'result': 'success', 'data': token}, allowing any attacker who intercepts the response or exploits an XSS to obtain the reset token without access to the victim's email.

OAuth callback returns distinct error messages for different account states: 'Account not found', 'Account is banned', and 'Workspace not found'. These differential responses allow attackers to enumerate valid accounts and determine their status by initiating OAuth flows with different email addresses.

Email register send endpoint returns the verification token in the response body. EmailRegisterSendEmailApi.post() returns {'result': 'success', 'data': token}. This token combined with brute-forcing the verification code could allow account takeover.

The forgot-password endpoint raises AuthenticationFailedError when the email is not found but returns a success response with a token when the account exists. This behavioral difference allows an attacker to enumerate valid email addresses by observing whether the response is an error or a success.

The email-code-login endpoint raises AuthenticationFailedError when the account is not found but returns a success token when the account exists. An attacker can enumerate valid emails by distinguishing error vs success responses on the POST /api/email-code-login endpoint.

Email verification code comparison uses Python's != operator (token_data['code'] != payload.code) which is not constant-time. An attacker can use timing differences to brute-force the verification code character by character, significantly reducing the search space from O(n^k) to O(n*k).

Password reset code comparison uses Python's != operator (payload.code != token_data.get('code')) which is not constant-time. An attacker could exploit timing differences to brute-force the reset code character by character.

The POST /api/login endpoint has no visible rate limiting on login attempts. While WebAppAuthService.authenticate may implement rate limiting internally, the controller-level code does not enforce it. This could allow brute-force password attacks. The forgot-password endpoint has IP-based rate limiting (is_email_send_ip_limit) but the login endpoint lacks equivalent protection.

The email-code-login send endpoint returns the verification token directly in the HTTP response body (return {'result': 'success', 'data': token}). Similarly, the forgot-password send endpoint returns its token in the response. If these tokens contain or can be used to derive the verification code, this weakens the email-based verification since an attacker who intercepts the response does not need access to the victim's email.

The forgot-password validity check endpoint returns a new token along with the email address in the response body. The new_token is generated for the 'reset' phase and returned to the client. If this token is predictable or if the response is intercepted, an attacker could use it to reset an arbitrary user's password without email access.

The client-supplied MIME type (file.mimetype from the multipart form) is passed directly to FileService.upload_file() and stored in the database without any server-side content-type detection. An attacker can upload a malicious executable with mimetype='image/png' to bypass any downstream MIME-based access controls or Content-Type headers served to other users.

File extension validation in FileService.upload_file() uses a configurable blacklist (UPLOAD_FILE_EXTENSION_BLACKLIST) rather than a strict allowlist. Any extension not explicitly blacklisted is permitted, meaning novel dangerous extensions (e.g., .hta, .cpl, .scr, .ps1, .jar, .elf) may be uploadable if the blacklist is incomplete. For non-'datasets' source uploads, there is no allowlist check at all beyond the blacklist.

No magic-byte or content-based file type validation is performed on uploaded files. The file content is stored as-is after only checking the extension and size. An attacker can upload a file with an innocuous extension (e.g., .png) but containing malicious content (e.g., an HTML file with embedded JavaScript, a polyglot PDF/JS file, or a web shell) which could be exploited if served or processed downstream.

In the workflow file validation logic, files with FileTransferMethod.TOOL_FILE bypass all validation checks unconditionally (allowed_file_types, allowed_file_extensions, transfer_methods). If an attacker can control or influence the transfer_method field to be TOOL_FILE, they can upload arbitrary file types that would otherwise be rejected by the configured FileUploadConfig. The FIXME comment in the code acknowledges this as a known issue.

Extension extraction uses os.path.splitext() which only returns the last extension. A filename like 'malware.php.png' yields extension 'png', passing the blacklist check while the original filename (stored in the database name field) retains the .php component. If any downstream system reconstructs the file path from the stored name rather than the UUID-based storage key, the double extension could lead to execution. Current storage is UUID-based which mitigates direct exploitation, but the stored filename could be used in download Content-Disposition headers.

The GetRemoteFileInfo endpoint accepts a user-controlled URL in the path and issues HEAD/GET requests via ssrf_proxy. SSRF protection relies entirely on external Squid proxy configuration. If the proxy is misconfigured, absent, or bypassed (e.g., via DNS rebinding), an attacker can probe internal services (169.254.169.254 cloud metadata, localhost services, internal APIs). The URL is taken directly from the path parameter with only URL-decoding applied.

The RemoteFileInfoApi (web) endpoint has the same SSRF pattern as the console variant. A user-supplied URL path parameter is decoded and used to make HEAD/GET requests. The web endpoint is accessible to end users (not just console admins), widening the attack surface. Protection depends on external proxy infrastructure.

The console RemoteFileUploadPayload defines url as a bare 'str' field with no URL scheme validation. An attacker could supply file://, gopher://, or other non-HTTP schemes. While httpx may reject some schemes, the lack of application-level validation means the defense is implicit and fragile. The web endpoint uses Pydantic HttpUrl which enforces http/https schemes, but the console endpoint does not. This inconsistency suggests the console endpoint was not hardened intentionally.

The get_remote_file_info() factory helper in remote.py issues HEAD requests to arbitrary URLs using ssrf_proxy for metadata resolution. This function is used in workflow file normalization paths, meaning any workflow that processes remote file references will trigger server-side requests. The URL is not validated for scheme or destination before the request is made.

Both upload endpoints perform a HEAD request to check file metadata (size, type), then a separate GET request to download content. Between these two requests, the remote resource could change — an attacker could serve a small benign file for HEAD, then a large malicious payload for GET, bypassing size and type validation. The console endpoint line 'content = resp.content if resp.request.method == "GET" else ssrf_proxy.get(url).content' explicitly shows the double-fetch pattern.

Error messages in both upload endpoints include the user-supplied URL and the response text from the remote server (resp.text). If an attacker targets an internal service, the error response body may leak internal service information, headers, or error pages back to the attacker. Example: 'raise RemoteFileUploadError(f"Failed to fetch file from {url}: {resp.text}")' exposes the response body of internal services.

User-controlled 'inputs' dict and 'query' string from the JSON request body are passed directly to AppGenerateService.generate() without any sanitization or prompt-injection mitigation at the controller layer. These values flow into prompt templates, allowing an attacker to manipulate LLM behavior via crafted input, potentially exfiltrating system prompts or invoking unintended tool calls.

Workflow run endpoint passes user-controlled 'inputs' dict directly to AppGenerateService.generate(). Workflow inputs are interpolated into prompt templates and may control tool parameters, enabling prompt injection that could alter workflow execution paths or exfiltrate data through tool invocations.

The 'files' parameter accepts a list of arbitrary dicts (list[dict[str, Any]]) from user input and passes them into the generation pipeline. If file URLs or references are resolved server-side without allowlist validation, an attacker could supply internal network URLs to perform SSRF attacks. The controller performs no validation on file contents or URLs.

WorkflowTaskStopApi explicitly uses AppQueueManager.set_stop_flag_no_user_check(task_id), which does not verify that the requesting user owns the task being stopped. Any authenticated API token holder for the same app can stop another user's workflow execution by guessing or enumerating task IDs. This is a horizontal privilege escalation (CWE-862: Missing Authorization).

WorkflowRunByIdApi accepts 'workflow_id' from the URL path and injects it directly into the args dict (args['workflow_id'] = workflow_id) without any format validation. While downstream services may validate this, the controller layer should enforce expected format (e.g., UUID) to prevent injection of unexpected values into the generation pipeline.

Exception descriptions from ProviderTokenNotInitError, InvokeError, and InvokeRateLimitError are propagated directly to API error responses (e.g., raise ProviderNotInitializeError(ex.description), raise CompletionRequestError(e.description)). These internal error messages may reveal provider configuration details, model names, rate limit thresholds, or internal infrastructure information to API consumers.

The 'query' field from ChatMessagePayload and CompletionMessagePayload is passed directly into AppGenerateService.generate() via the 'args' dict, which feeds it into LLM prompt construction. No sanitization, content filtering, or prompt-injection defenses are applied at the controller layer. An attacker can craft query strings that manipulate the system prompt, extract confidential instructions, or cause the LLM to perform unintended actions.

WorkflowRunPayload.inputs is a dict[str, Any] that is passed directly into workflow execution. These values are interpolated into workflow node variables (e.g., HTTP request URLs, code execution nodes, database queries) without type checking or allowlist validation at the controller layer. An attacker can inject payloads that are interpreted by downstream workflow nodes, potentially achieving SSRF, code execution, or data exfiltration depending on the workflow configuration.

The 'files' parameter in CompletionMessagePayload, ChatMessagePayload, and WorkflowRunPayload accepts list[dict[str, Any]] with no schema validation on file type, size, content-type, or allowed extensions at the controller layer. Malicious file metadata could be used to trigger unexpected behavior in downstream file processing services (e.g., SSRF via URL-type files, path traversal via filenames, or resource exhaustion via large uploads).

The 'inputs' field is typed as dict[str, Any] in all three payload models (CompletionMessagePayload, ChatMessagePayload, WorkflowRunPayload). There is no allowlist of expected keys, no type constraints on values, and no length limits. This allows an attacker to pass arbitrary data (deeply nested objects, very large strings, unexpected types) which may cause downstream issues including denial of service, type confusion in variable interpolation, or injection attacks in template rendering.

Multiple exception handlers forward ex.description directly to the HTTP response (e.g., ProviderNotInitializeError(ex.description), CompletionRequestError(e.description)). These descriptions may contain internal provider names, model identifiers, rate limit details, or stack information that aids an attacker in reconnaissance. The bare 'raise e' for ValueError also leaks internal validation messages.

ValueError exceptions are re-raised directly ('raise e') without wrapping, exposing internal validation error messages to end users. In completion.py this pattern appears in both CompletionApi.post and ChatApi.post. These messages may reveal internal field names, expected formats, or configuration details useful for targeted attacks.

User-supplied code is injected into the runner script via simple string replacement (script.replace(cls._code_placeholder, code)) in assemble_runner_script(). There is no AST validation, allow-listing, or sanitization of the code parameter before it is concatenated into the executable script. An attacker who controls the 'code' input can inject arbitrary Python code that runs outside the intended main() function boundary, potentially importing dangerous modules or performing operations the sandbox does not restrict.

The Python3 runner script embeds user code directly at the top of the generated script via the {{code}} placeholder. The user code executes in the same Python process as the runner harness. Since the code is placed before the harness logic (import json, b64decode, etc.), a malicious user can define code that overrides built-in modules, monkey-patches json.loads, or executes arbitrary side effects at import time before the main() call. No AST transformation or restriction is applied to the user code.

The Node.js runner script embeds user code directly at the top of the generated script via the {{code}} placeholder. User-supplied JavaScript is concatenated without validation. A malicious user can require('child_process').execSync() or access the file system via require('fs'), access environment variables, or perform other Node.js-level operations that the sandbox may not fully restrict. The code runs with full Node.js capabilities available in the sandbox container.

The Jinja2 transformer uses jinja2.Template (the default unsandboxed environment) to render user-controlled templates. Although the code runs inside the remote sandbox service, the Jinja2 template itself is rendered with full Jinja2 capabilities. An attacker can craft a template using Jinja2 introspection (e.g., {{''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__()}}) to escape the template engine and execute arbitrary Python code within the sandbox process. The jinja2.SandboxedEnvironment is NOT used. This is a classic Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) vector.

The execute_code() method sends 'enable_network': True to the sandbox service for all code executions. This allows user-supplied code running in the sandbox to make outbound network requests. Combined with the ability to execute arbitrary code (RCE-002/RCE-004/RCE-005), an attacker can exfiltrate data, perform SSRF attacks against internal infrastructure, download additional payloads, or establish reverse shells. Network access should be disabled by default and only enabled when explicitly required.

Because enable_network=True is hardcoded in execute_code(), any code running in the sandbox can reach internal network endpoints. The sandbox service endpoint itself (CODE_EXECUTION_ENDPOINT) is likely on an internal network. User-supplied code can probe internal services, access cloud metadata endpoints (e.g., 169.254.169.254), or reach other services that trust the sandbox's network position. This is a direct SSRF vector when combined with arbitrary code execution.

The CodeExecutor relies entirely on the external sandbox service for security isolation. The application itself performs zero validation, sanitization, or restriction on the code before sending it to the sandbox. If the sandbox service is misconfigured, has vulnerabilities, or is bypassed, there is no defense-in-depth. The API key for the sandbox (CODE_EXECUTION_API_KEY) is the sole authentication mechanism. A compromised or poorly-configured sandbox service would result in full RCE on the sandbox host.

Error messages from the sandbox execution (response_data.get('message'), response_code.data.error) are propagated back through CodeExecutionError exceptions. These may contain stack traces, file paths, environment details, or other internal information from the sandbox that could aid an attacker in crafting more targeted exploits.

upload_pkg defaults verify_signature=False, allowing installation of unsigned plugin packages. An attacker compromising the plugin distribution channel can inject arbitrary code. The same issue exists in upload_bundle.

PluginAppBackwardsInvocation.invoke_app allows any plugin to invoke any app in the tenant with SERVICE_API privilege. No per-app permission check or plugin-scope restriction is enforced. A malicious plugin can invoke admin-level workflows, exfiltrate data from other apps, or trigger destructive operations.

PluginToolBackwardsInvocation.invoke_tool accepts arbitrary tool_type, provider, tool_name, and tool_parameters from plugin context with no allowlist or scope restriction. A malicious plugin can invoke any registered tool (including code execution, HTTP request, or file system tools) to achieve remote code execution or server-side request forgery.

PluginEncrypter.invoke_encrypt exposes decrypt operation to plugins, allowing a malicious plugin to decrypt arbitrary provider credentials by specifying the target namespace and identity. This leaks API keys and secrets for all configured providers in the tenant.

deserialize_request parses raw HTTP bytes without validating header names/values for CRLF injection, without verifying Content-Length matches actual body length, and without restricting sensitive headers. An attacker can inject arbitrary headers or craft malformed requests that are interpreted differently by upstream and downstream components.

The Host header from untrusted raw HTTP bytes is used to populate SERVER_NAME and SERVER_PORT in the WSGI environ without validation. This allows manipulation of server-side URL generation, routing decisions, and can bypass host-based access controls.

invoke_app allows plugins to specify an arbitrary user_id to impersonate any user in the tenant. When user_id is empty, it auto-creates an EndUser, and when provided, it performs a database lookup without verifying the calling plugin's authorization to act as that user. This enables cross-user data access and action impersonation.

upload_pkg accepts arbitrary bytes with no application-level size limit and signature verification disabled by default. This enables denial-of-service via oversized uploads and supply-chain attacks via malicious package content that passes no integrity check.

The MCP endpoint POST /mcp/server/<server_code>/mcp has no authentication decorator or middleware. The only access control is knowledge of the server_code string. Any party that discovers or brute-forces a valid server_code can invoke all MCP methods including tool execution. This violates CWE-306 (Missing Authentication for Critical Function).

In handle_mcp_request(), the generic Exception handler returns 'Internal server error: ' + str(e) in the JSON-RPC error response. This leaks internal exception details (stack traces, file paths, SQL errors) to unauthenticated callers. The ValueError handler similarly exposes str(e). CWE-209 (Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information).

handle_call_tool() passes user-supplied arguments from the unauthenticated MCP CallToolRequest directly to AppGenerateService.generate() with InvokeFrom.SERVICE_API privileges. Since the MCP endpoint lacks authentication, an attacker can trigger arbitrary app execution including workflow runs, LLM invocations, and tool calls. The arguments dict is only minimally validated (mode-based key extraction) with no sanitization. Combined with AUTH-BYPASS, this enables remote code execution through tool invocation.

In auth_flow.py, discover_protected_resource_metadata() and discover_oauth_authorization_server_metadata() construct discovery URLs from server_url which may be partially user-controlled (via MCP provider configuration). The functions build multiple fallback URLs including well-known paths and make HTTP GET requests via ssrf_proxy. While ssrf_proxy provides some SSRF protection, the URL construction logic in build_protected_resource_metadata_discovery_urls() accepts a www_auth_resource_metadata_url parameter that undergoes normalization but could still be manipulated to target internal services if the URL validation is bypassed.

The _get_mcp_server_and_app() and _validate_server_status() methods return distinct error messages: 'Server Not Found', 'App Not Found', and 'Server is not active'. These differential responses allow an unauthenticated attacker to enumerate valid server_code values and determine whether associated apps exist. CWE-204 (Observable Response Discrepancy).

In _handle_mcp_request(), when an InitializeRequest is received and no end_user exists, a new EndUser record is created using client-supplied clientInfo.name and clientInfo.version with no authentication. An attacker can create arbitrary EndUser records by sending InitializeRequest messages with crafted client names to any active MCP server. The client_name is formatted as '{name}@{version}' and stored directly without sanitization, enabling potential injection of misleading user identities.

In AgentNode._extract_variable_selector_to_variable_mapping, user-controlled agent_parameters values of type 'mixed' or 'constant' are passed to VariableTemplateParser(str(input.value)). If the variable template syntax permits expression evaluation or cross-context variable references, a malicious workflow author can inject variable selectors that reference variables from other workflow contexts, leaking data between executions. The same pattern exists in DatasourceNode._extract_variable_selector_to_variable_mapping for 'mixed' type parameters.

In KnowledgeRetrievalNode._resolve_metadata_filtering_conditions (lines 286-300), metadata filtering condition values are passed through variable_pool.convert_template() without sanitization. If a user-controlled input is used as a metadata filtering condition value, the template engine will resolve variable references, potentially allowing an attacker to extract variables from the workflow runtime pool. This could leak sensitive context such as API keys, conversation data, or system variables stored in the pool.

TriggerWebhookNode._extract_configured_outputs unconditionally includes the complete raw webhook payload as outputs['_webhook_raw'] (line 178). This exposes all HTTP headers (including Authorization, Cookie, and other sensitive headers), query parameters, and body content to every downstream workflow node. Any node in the DAG can read this data, violating the principle of least privilege. An attacker who can add nodes to the workflow or control variable references downstream can exfiltrate credentials and session tokens passed in the original webhook request.

Multiple nodes propagate raw exception messages to workflow outputs via str(e). In AgentNode._run (line 90, 131), DatasourceNode._run (lines 171, 181), and KnowledgeRetrievalNode._run (lines 139, 149, 158), internal error details including stack context, plugin paths, and configuration details are exposed. These error messages may reveal internal infrastructure details to workflow consumers.

In DatasourceNode._run (line 141-142), for LOCAL_FILE type, the file_id is resolved from datasource_info using resolve_file_record_id(datasource_info.get('reference') or datasource_info.get('related_id')). The datasource_info originates from the variable pool's SystemVariableKey.DATASOURCE_INFO which is populated from external input. There is no visible authorization check verifying the requesting user/tenant actually owns the referenced file before it is retrieved via get_upload_file_by_id. An attacker who can manipulate the datasource_info dict could reference arbitrary file IDs to access files belonging to other tenants.

In TriggerWebhookNode.generate_file_var (lines 78-101), file references from the webhook payload body are resolved via resolve_file_record_id without verifying that the webhook caller is authorized to access the referenced file. The file dict comes directly from webhook_data which is externally supplied. An attacker sending a crafted webhook payload with arbitrary file references could cause the workflow to load and process files belonging to other users or tenants.

In KnowledgeRetrievalNode._fetch_dataset_retriever, dataset_ids from node_data.dataset_ids are passed directly to the retrieval layer without visible tenant-scoped authorization. While node_data is typically configured by a workflow author (who should only have access to their own datasets), if the dataset_ids can be manipulated (e.g., through workflow import or API manipulation), it could allow cross-tenant dataset access. The tenant_id is passed alongside but authorization enforcement depends on the downstream DatasetRetrieval implementation.

The webhook endpoint at /triggers/webhook/<webhook_id> accepts GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, HEAD, and OPTIONS methods without any authentication or authorization checks. Any external caller who knows or guesses a webhook_id can invoke the associated workflow. The only gate is knowledge of the webhook_id string, which is passed directly to WebhookService.get_webhook_trigger_and_workflow() for a database lookup. There is no HMAC signature verification, no shared secret validation, and no IP allowlisting.

Webhook IDs serve as the sole authorization token for triggering workflows. The webhook_id is a plain string path parameter looked up directly in the database (WorkflowWebhookTrigger.webhook_id == webhook_id). If webhook IDs are predictable (e.g., sequential integers, short UUIDs, or low-entropy tokens), an attacker can enumerate valid IDs and trigger arbitrary tenant workflows. The lack of additional authentication means possession of the ID equals authorization.

The handle_webhook exception handler catches all exceptions and returns the raw exception message to the caller via str(e) in the JSON response body: `jsonify({"error": "Internal server error", "message": str(e)}), 500`. This can leak internal implementation details such as database table names, SQL error messages, file paths, or service configuration to unauthenticated external callers. The debug endpoint (handle_webhook_debug) correctly returns a generic message instead.

The debug webhook endpoint at /triggers/webhook-debug/<webhook_id> is also unauthenticated. When no active debug listener is registered, the 409 response includes the production webhook URL via `webhook_trigger.webhook_url` in the `execution_url` field. This leaks the production trigger URL to any unauthenticated caller who discovers a debug webhook ID, potentially enabling them to invoke the production workflow.

Neither the production webhook endpoint nor the debug webhook endpoint implements rate limiting at the controller or decorator level. While backend AppTriggerStatus.RATE_LIMITED status exists as an account-level quota mechanism, there is no per-IP or per-webhook request rate limiting to prevent brute-force enumeration of webhook IDs or denial-of-service via high-volume webhook invocations that consume workflow execution resources.

The plugin_inner_api_only decorator authenticates requests using a static shared key (INNER_API_KEY_FOR_PLUGIN) compared via simple string equality from the X-Inner-Api-Key header. There is no network-layer restriction (IP allowlist, mTLS, etc.) enforced in the application. If this API is accidentally exposed beyond the internal network — through misconfigured reverse proxy, cloud load balancer, or container networking — any attacker with the key (or ability to brute-force it) gains full access to invoke arbitrary LLM models, tools, apps, encryption/decryption, and file upload signing on any tenant.

The get_user_tenant decorator in plugin/wraps.py extracts tenant_id and user_id directly from the JSON request body and uses them to look up the tenant and user without verifying that the authenticated caller (plugin daemon) is authorized to act on behalf of that specific tenant. A compromised or malicious plugin can specify any tenant_id to invoke LLM models, tools, or apps belonging to an arbitrary tenant. The code comment explicitly acknowledges: 'user_id is not trusted, it could be maliciously set to any value'. If user_id does not exist, a new EndUser is silently created for the target tenant.

All plugin invoke endpoints (LLM, tool, rerank, TTS, speech2text, moderation, app, summary, text-embedding, parameter-extractor, question-classifier) have no rate limiting, request size limits, or quota enforcement at the API layer. A compromised plugin daemon key allows unlimited invocations of expensive LLM operations across all tenants, enabling resource exhaustion and cost amplification attacks.

The mail endpoints (enterprise/mail and billing/mail) accept arbitrary recipient list, subject, and body from the caller, then dispatch emails via send_inner_email_task with no rate limiting, recipient validation, or content sanitization. An attacker with the inner API key can use these endpoints as an open email relay for phishing or spam, sending arbitrary content to any email address. The substitutions parameter is passed directly to the email template engine, which may allow template injection depending on implementation.

Multiple plugin invoke endpoints catch broad Exception and return str(e) in the response body via BaseBackwardsInvocationResponse(error=str(e)). This can leak internal implementation details such as database connection strings, file paths, model provider error messages, and stack trace fragments to the caller.

The enterprise workspace creation endpoint accepts an owner_email in the request body and assigns that account as workspace owner without verifying that the caller has any relationship with or authority over the specified email address. An attacker with the enterprise inner API key can create workspaces and assign ownership to any existing account, potentially granting the target user unintended access or creating confusion about workspace ownership hierarchies.

WordExtractor fetches external image URLs embedded in DOCX documents via ssrf_proxy.get(url). A malicious DOCX file can contain external image references pointing to internal network resources (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/). While ssrf_proxy is used, the URL is derived from the document's rel.target_ref without additional validation or allowlist filtering. An attacker can craft a DOCX with OLE relationships pointing to internal services, cloud metadata endpoints, or other sensitive infrastructure.

ExtractProcessor.load_from_url() accepts a URL parameter and fetches it via ssrf_proxy.get(). The URL is used to download content which is then processed by file extractors based on the Content-Type or file extension derived from the response. If the URL or redirect target points to internal services, the server will fetch internal resources. The suffix/extension is derived from Content-Type header or Content-Disposition filename via regex, which can be manipulated by the remote server to influence which extractor handles the content.

UnstructuredXmlExtractor calls partition_xml(filename=self._file_path, xml_keep_tags=True) on user-uploaded XML files. The unstructured library's XML parser may not disable external entity resolution by default, potentially allowing XXE attacks. A malicious XML file with external entity declarations could read local files (e.g., /etc/passwd) or trigger SSRF. The xml_keep_tags=True parameter preserves XML structure including potentially dangerous entity references.

UnstructuredEpubExtractor calls pypandoc.download_pandoc() at extraction time, which downloads and installs a pandoc binary from the internet. This is called in the code path for processing user-uploaded EPUB files. If the download source is compromised (supply chain attack) or a MITM occurs, a malicious pandoc binary could be executed. Additionally, pandoc itself processes complex document formats that have historically been vectors for code execution.

Multiple extractors process complex binary file formats (PDF via pypdfium2, DOCX via python-docx, XLSX via openpyxl, PPT/PPTX via unstructured) on user-uploaded content without sandboxing. These parsing libraries handle complex formats with known historical CVEs. A specially crafted document could exploit a parser vulnerability to achieve remote code execution. The extract_processor.py dispatches to these parsers based solely on file extension, meaning an attacker controls which parser handles their malicious payload.

In ExtractProcessor.load_from_url(), the file suffix is extracted from the URL path via Path(url).suffix or from the Content-Disposition header filename via regex. The suffix is concatenated directly into a temp file path without sanitization: file_path = f"{temp_dir}/{next(tempfile._get_candidate_names())}{suffix}". Although the file is within a temp directory, a crafted suffix containing path separators or null bytes could potentially escape the temp directory on certain OS/filesystem configurations.

WordExtractor accepts a file_path in the constructor and uses os.path.expanduser() on it if it contains '~'. If the file_path is user-controlled, tilde expansion could resolve to unexpected home directories. Additionally, the _is_valid_url() check only validates URL structure, and if the path is not a local file, it attempts to download it via ssrf_proxy without validating the destination. The downloaded content is written to a NamedTemporaryFile and processed.

CacheEmbedding uses pickle.dumps() to serialize embedding vectors and pickle.loads() (via embedding.get_embedding()) to deserialize them from the database. While the embeddings are generated server-side, if an attacker gains write access to the Embedding table (e.g., via SQL injection in another component), they could store a malicious pickle payload that executes arbitrary code when deserialized during embedding cache lookups. The pickle protocol is used at HIGHEST_PROTOCOL level.

NotionExtractor makes outbound HTTP requests to Notion API endpoints using httpx.post() and httpx.request() without using the ssrf_proxy helper. While these requests target api.notion.com, the notion_obj_id parameter is user-controlled and is interpolated into URL templates (BLOCK_CHILD_URL_TMPL, DATABASE_URL_TMPL, etc.). The block data fetching recursively follows has_children references, meaning the Notion API response controls further request targets. If the Notion API token has broad access, this could be used to exfiltrate data from unintended Notion workspaces.

CacheEmbedding.embed_query() logs the first 10 characters of user query text when DEBUG mode is enabled: logger.exception("Failed to embed query text '%s...(%s chars)'", text[:10], len(text)). Similarly, error paths in embed_documents expose processing details. This could leak sensitive user query content into log files accessible to operators or log aggregation systems.

The UnstructuredWordExtractor, UnstructuredPPTXExtractor, UnstructuredEpubExtractor, and UnstructuredEmailExtractor all call partition_via_api() which sends the uploaded file content to an external unstructured API endpoint configured via dify_config.UNSTRUCTURED_API_URL. This URL is server-configured but the file content (which is user-controlled) is sent to this endpoint. If the UNSTRUCTURED_API_URL is misconfigured or points to an attacker-controlled server, user document content would be exfiltrated. The API key is also transmitted with each request.

The website crawl endpoint accepts a user-supplied URL and passes it directly to WebsiteService.crawl_url() via WebsiteCrawlApiRequest without server-side URL validation or allowlist enforcement. An authenticated user can supply internal/private network URLs (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/, http://localhost:6379/) to scan internal services, read cloud metadata, or pivot within the network. The provider field is limited to three values but the url field has no restrictions.

The document-create-by-file endpoint (service API) reads file content from the multipart upload and passes it to FileService.upload_file() using the client-supplied filename and mimetype. The filename is taken directly from file.filename without sanitization for path traversal characters. The mimetype is client-controlled. Although FileService may perform some checks, the controller itself does not validate file extension against an allowlist, does not verify the actual content type matches the declared mimetype, and does not restrict filename characters. Malicious filenames (e.g., containing '../') or specially crafted files (polyglots) could exploit the downstream extraction pipeline.

In the service API document-create-by-file endpoint, arbitrary JSON from request.form['data'] is parsed via json.loads() and then merged into args dict without field-level filtering. This dict is passed to KnowledgeConfig.model_validate(args) which constructs the full knowledge configuration. An attacker can inject unexpected fields (e.g., original_document_id, embedding_model_provider, data_source overrides) into the configuration. While Pydantic will ignore truly unknown fields, any fields that match the model schema but are not intended to be user-settable (like original_document_id) can be overridden.

In the console API DatasetDocumentListApi.post(), the entire console_ns.payload is passed to KnowledgeConfig.model_validate() without explicit field filtering. The KnowledgeConfig model is a broad internal configuration type and any field matching its schema can be set by the caller. This allows an API consumer to set internal-only fields if they exist in the Pydantic model schema, potentially overriding data_source type, embedding model settings, or other sensitive configuration.

In the service API DocumentListApi.get(), user-supplied keyword is interpolated into a LIKE pattern using f-string formatting: search = f'%{query_params.keyword}%'. While SQLAlchemy parameterizes the value preventing classic SQL injection, the LIKE wildcards ('%' and '_') within the user input are not escaped. An attacker can craft keyword values containing '%' or '_' to manipulate query matching behavior, potentially causing denial of service through expensive wildcard scans or extracting information about document names through targeted pattern matching.

Same LIKE wildcard injection pattern in the console API DatasetDocumentListApi.get(). User search input is wrapped with '%' delimiters without escaping SQL LIKE meta-characters: search = f'%{search}%'. This allows manipulation of pattern matching behavior.

In the service API DocumentAddByFileApi.post() (line 399-403), the document is saved using dataset.created_by_account as the acting account rather than current_user. This means the uploaded document is attributed to the dataset creator, not the API caller. While tenant_id is checked on the dataset lookup, an API token holder who has access to any dataset within the tenant could upload documents attributed to the dataset owner. The same pattern appears in DocumentUpdateByFileApi.post() at line 496-503.

The website crawl endpoint catches broad Exception and re-raises it as WebsiteCrawlError(str(e)), which may expose internal error details such as internal hostnames, network topology, stack traces, or third-party API error messages to the client. This is especially dangerous when combined with SSRF, as connection errors to internal services reveal their existence.

The signature verification in verify_plugin_file_signature checks that current_time - int(timestamp) <= FILES_ACCESS_TIMEOUT but does not track used nonces. An attacker who intercepts a valid signed URL can replay the exact same request (timestamp, nonce, sign) unlimited times within the timeout window. No nonce-consumption or deduplication mechanism exists.

The HMAC secret key falls back to an empty byte string (b"") when dify_config.SECRET_KEY is not set: `secret_key = dify_config.SECRET_KEY.encode() if dify_config.SECRET_KEY else b""`. With an empty key, any attacker can forge valid HMAC signatures for arbitrary file uploads. This is a configuration-dependent vulnerability but the code does not fail-closed when the secret is absent.

The tenant_id parameter is supplied by the caller in query string args and passed directly to get_user() and ToolFileManager.create_file_by_raw(). Although the signature binds tenant_id, the signature itself is generated server-side for plugins—meaning a compromised or malicious plugin can use its valid signed URL to upload files under any tenant context as long as it possesses the signed parameters. The upload endpoint performs no independent authorization check (e.g., verifying the caller actually belongs to the tenant).

The endpoint trusts the client-supplied MIME type (file.mimetype) and filename (file.filename) from the multipart upload without validating the actual file content against the declared type. While the signature binds filename and mimetype, this only proves the server previously approved those values—it does not prevent content-type mismatch. An attacker could upload a file whose content does not match its declared MIME type (e.g., an executable disguised as an image), potentially leading to stored XSS or other content-type confusion attacks when the file is served.

The get_website_crawl method in WebsiteCrawlDatasourcePlugin forwards user-supplied datasource_parameters (which contain the target URL) directly to the plugin daemon via PluginDatasourceManager without any URL validation, scheme restriction, or SSRF protection. There is no check against internal/private IP ranges (e.g., 169.254.169.254, 10.0.0.0/8, 127.0.0.0/8), no URL scheme allowlist (e.g., restricting to http/https), and no DNS rebinding protection. An attacker who can control the crawl URL can probe internal services, access cloud metadata endpoints, or reach other internal infrastructure.

PluginDatasourceManager.get_website_crawl dispatches user-controlled datasource_parameters to the plugin daemon HTTP endpoint without any server-side URL validation or SSRF mitigation. The parameters are serialized directly into the POST body sent to the plugin daemon. Even if individual plugins implement their own validation, the platform layer provides no defense-in-depth. A malicious or misconfigured plugin, or a plugin that trusts the platform to validate inputs, would be fully exposed to SSRF attacks targeting internal networks and cloud metadata services.

datasource_parameters is typed as Mapping[str, Any] and passed through multiple layers (WebsiteCrawlDatasourcePlugin -> PluginDatasourceManager -> plugin daemon) without any schema validation, type checking, or sanitization at the platform level. The WebsiteCrawlDatasourcePlugin.get_website_crawl method accepts arbitrary key-value pairs and forwards them unchanged. This lack of input validation at the orchestration layer means that any unexpected or malicious parameters (e.g., extra fields that override plugin behavior, excessively large payloads) will reach the plugin daemon unchecked.

The WebsiteCrawlDatasourcePluginProviderController.get_datasource method constructs a WebsiteCrawlDatasourcePlugin using entity data looked up by user-supplied datasource_name. While the lookup itself uses an equality check against known entities, the returned plugin object carries runtime credentials that will be forwarded to the plugin daemon alongside unvalidated user parameters. The provider layer performs no validation of the datasource_parameters that will eventually be passed to the plugin, creating a gap where the controller trusts downstream components to validate inputs that they also do not validate.

The 'filename' parameter in save/load_once/load_stream/download/delete methods across all storage backends is passed directly to cloud storage APIs (S3 Key, Azure blob name, GCS blob name, etc.) without any sanitization. An attacker who controls the filename can use sequences like '../' or absolute paths to read/write/delete arbitrary objects in the storage bucket. None of the 7 storage backends perform path normalization, prefix-bounding, or validation on the filename.

Azure Blob Storage save/load/download/delete methods pass the filename directly to Azure SDK blob operations without path sanitization. An attacker controlling the filename can traverse to arbitrary blobs in the container.
Google Cloud Storage operations use the filename directly as the blob name without validation. Path traversal characters in the filename can target arbitrary objects in the GCS bucket.

Aliyun OSS uses posixpath.join(self.folder, filename) which does NOT prevent path traversal. If filename is an absolute path (e.g. '/etc/passwd') or contains '../' sequences, posixpath.join will resolve to a path outside the intended folder prefix, allowing access to arbitrary objects in the bucket.
Tencent COS storage passes filename directly as the Key parameter without sanitization, allowing path traversal to arbitrary objects in the bucket.
Supabase storage passes filename directly to upload/download/delete methods without sanitization, allowing path traversal to arbitrary objects in the bucket.

OpenDAL storage passes filename directly to op.write/op.read/op.delete without sanitization. When scheme is 'fs' (local filesystem), this is especially dangerous as it enables reading/writing arbitrary files on the host filesystem.

The BaseStorage interface and all 7 implementations accept a raw filename string without any authorization context (user ID, tenant ID, session). Any caller that controls the filename can access any file in the bucket. The storage layer has no concept of ownership or access control — it relies entirely on the caller to construct safe filenames. If any upstream code path passes user-controlled input as the filename, it results in insecure direct object reference.

Azure Blob Storage caches the generated SAS token in Redis using a predictable key pattern 'azure_blob_sas_token_{account_name}_{account_key}'. The SAS token is stored as plaintext in Redis. Additionally, the cache key itself contains the account_key, which means the secret key is used as part of a Redis key visible to anyone with Redis access. If Redis is compromised, both the SAS token and the account key pattern are exposed.

The Azure SAS token is generated with very broad permissions: read, write, delete, list, add, create on all resource types (service, container, object). This violates the principle of least privilege. A leaked SAS token would grant full control over the storage account. The token should be scoped to only the specific container and operations needed.

The download() method in multiple backends (S3, Azure, GCS, Supabase, OpenDAL) writes to target_filepath without any validation. If an attacker controls target_filepath, they can write downloaded content to arbitrary locations on the filesystem. The Azure implementation uses open() directly; Supabase uses Path.write_bytes(); OpenDAL uses Path.write_bytes(). None validate that the target path is within an expected directory.

The exists() method in AwsS3Storage uses a bare 'except:' clause that catches all exceptions including KeyboardInterrupt and SystemExit. This silently swallows errors that could indicate security issues such as permission denied (403), which would incorrectly return False instead of raising an error. A 403 response might indicate a misconfigured bucket policy that should be investigated.

OpenDAL storage reads credentials from a .env file at runtime using dotenv_values(). This means sensitive cloud storage credentials (access keys, secret keys) may be stored in a .env file on disk. If the application is compromised via another vulnerability (e.g., the path traversal in this same storage layer), the .env file could be read to exfiltrate all cloud credentials.

The /credentials/validate endpoint accepts an arbitrary credentials dict (dict[str, Any]) and passes it directly to validate_provider_credentials(), which in turn calls the provider plugin's validation logic. If a provider plugin uses credential values (e.g., api_base_url, endpoint) to make outbound HTTP requests during validation, an attacker with a valid session can supply a malicious URL pointing to internal services, enabling SSRF. The credentials dict has no schema enforcement or URL allowlist at the controller layer.

The provider parameter is captured via <path:provider>, which permits path segments including slashes. While tenant_id is derived from the authenticated session, the provider string is not validated against an allowlist of known providers before being passed to _get_provider_configuration(). If the provider lookup logic has any path-based resolution or plugin loading, a crafted provider value could reference unintended providers or trigger unexpected behavior. The validate endpoint only requires @login_required and @account_initialization_required (not @is_admin_or_owner_required), giving any workspace member access.

The ParserCredentialValidate model defines credentials as dict[str, Any] with no field-level validation. Any JSON-serializable value (nested objects, large payloads, special characters) can be submitted. This unvalidated dict flows into provider_configuration.validate_provider_credentials(credentials), where plugin code consumes arbitrary keys and values. This could lead to injection attacks depending on how individual provider plugins process credential values (e.g., command injection if a plugin shells out, header injection if values are placed in HTTP headers).

The POST /console/api/workspaces/current/tool-provider/api/add endpoint accepts a user-supplied OpenAPI schema (payload.schema_) that defines arbitrary server URLs. These URLs are stored and later used by the system to make HTTP requests during workflow tool invocation. An authenticated admin/owner can register an API tool pointing to internal network addresses (e.g., http://169.254.169.254, http://localhost:8080) enabling SSRF attacks against cloud metadata services or internal infrastructure. The schema field is parsed by ApiBasedToolSchemaParser.auto_parse_to_tool_bundle() but no URL allowlist or blocklist validation is applied to the server URLs defined within the schema.

The GET /console/api/workspaces/current/tool-provider/api/remote endpoint in ToolApiProviderGetRemoteSchemaApi fetches a remote URL (query.url) server-side via httpx.get() in get_api_tool_provider_remote_schema(). Although pydantic HttpUrl validation constrains the URL format, there is no SSRF protection against internal/private IP ranges. An attacker can supply URLs targeting internal services (http://10.0.0.1, http://metadata.google.internal, http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/). The comment 'try to parse schema, avoid SSRF attack' in the service layer only validates the response is valid schema - it does not validate the target URL against internal networks.

The create_api_tool_provider() method in ApiToolManageService stores user-supplied OpenAPI/Swagger schema directly into the database (db_provider.schema = schema) after parsing it into tool bundles. The schema can define arbitrary API endpoint paths, HTTP methods, headers, and parameters. When these tools are later invoked during workflow execution, the system will construct and send HTTP requests based on these attacker-controlled definitions. There is no validation that the defined endpoints are safe or that the schema doesn't define operations that could be abused (e.g., PUT/DELETE methods against internal services).

An authenticated admin/owner can register a custom API tool provider that points to an attacker-controlled external server. When this tool is subsequently used in AI workflows or agent executions, conversation data, user inputs, and intermediate results can be sent as HTTP request parameters/body to the external server. The endpoint requires admin/owner role (@is_admin_or_owner_required) but a compromised admin account or malicious insider can leverage this for persistent data exfiltration. The credentials (including API keys) are encrypted before storage, but the tool invocation itself sends workflow data to the configured external endpoint.

The POST /api/form/human_input/<form_token> endpoint is intentionally unauthenticated (comment: 'this endpoint is unauthenticated on purpose for now'). The commented-out _end_user parameter means submission_end_user_id is always None, so there is no identity binding between the form and the submitter. Any party who obtains or guesses a form_token can submit the form and trigger workflow continuation (resume_app_execution). This defeats authorization controls on workflow state transitions.

The rate limiter keys on the client IP obtained via extract_remote_ip(), which trusts the X-Forwarded-For and CF-Connecting-IP headers without validation. An attacker can rotate these header values to bypass the rate limit entirely, enabling brute-force enumeration of form tokens or repeated submission attempts. The rate limiter is the only protective control on this unauthenticated endpoint.

Form tokens are the sole authorization gate for both GET (reading form definitions including rendered content) and POST (submitting input and resuming workflows). If tokens are insufficiently random (e.g., UUIDs without cryptographic randomness) or leaked via logs/referrer headers, any unauthenticated user can access or submit arbitrary forms. The service layer (get_form_by_token) performs no caller identity check beyond token possession.

A successful POST submission by an unauthenticated attacker calls submit_form_by_token which invokes enqueue_resume → resume_app_execution.apply_async, continuing a paused workflow. Because submission_end_user_id is hardcoded to None, there is no audit trail of who actually submitted the form. An attacker with a valid form_token can approve or reject workflow steps, potentially authorizing actions (e.g., financial approvals, deployments) without legitimate user consent.

The tenant-isolated task queue deserializes task data from Redis using DocumentTask(**next_task) without schema validation. If an attacker gains access to the Redis queue (e.g., via SSRF or misconfigured network), they could inject malicious task payloads that alter dataset_id/document_ids to process arbitrary documents or cause exceptions that disrupt the queue processing pipeline.

delete_account_task accepts an account_id parameter and deletes the account from billing and sends a success email without verifying that the caller is authorized to delete that account. The task trusts the account_id passed to it entirely. If the Celery queue is compromised or an internal service dispatches a deletion with a wrong ID, any account can be deleted. The billing deletion also proceeds before verifying the account exists in the database, meaning billing state can be corrupted for non-existent accounts.

document_indexing_task and its variants accept dataset_id and document_ids as trusted parameters without verifying that the requesting tenant actually owns the dataset. While document lookup filters by dataset_id, there is no cross-tenant authorization check ensuring that tenant_id matches the dataset's owning tenant. A compromised or misbehaving queue message could cause documents from one tenant's dataset to be re-indexed or manipulated by referencing another tenant's dataset_id.

In delete_account_task, the billing service deletion (BillingService.delete_account) is executed before verifying the account exists in the local database. If the account does not exist, billing state is already mutated but the task returns early without sending a confirmation email, leaving an inconsistent state. Additionally, the billing deletion and account lookup are not atomic, creating a TOCTOU window where the account could be deleted between billing service call and local lookup.

rag_pipeline_run_task retrieves serialized pipeline invoke entities from file storage via FileService.get_file_content() and deserializes them with json.loads() followed by model_validate on each entity. The file content is fetched using a file ID passed as a task argument. If an attacker can control the file_id parameter (via queue poisoning or SSRF to the task broker), they could point to a crafted file containing malicious pipeline configurations that execute arbitrary workflows, access unauthorized tenants, or cause resource exhaustion through large payloads.

In run_single_rag_pipeline_task, the user_id and tenant_id are extracted from the deserialized pipeline entity and used to load an Account object which is then set as the current user via g._login_user. The pipeline generator is then invoked with InvokeFrom.PUBLISHED_PIPELINE. There is no validation that user_id is authorized to act on behalf of tenant_id, or that the pipeline_id belongs to that tenant. An attacker who can inject entities into the file storage or poison the queue can impersonate any user in any tenant and execute arbitrary pipeline workflows.

Both document_indexing_task and rag_pipeline_run_task use TenantIsolatedTaskQueue.pull_tasks() to retrieve the next batch of tasks from Redis and immediately dispatch them without any integrity or authenticity check on the queue contents. If Redis is compromised or accessible without authentication, an attacker can inject arbitrary task payloads into the tenant isolation queue, leading to unauthorized document processing, pipeline execution, or denial of service through queue flooding.

rag_pipeline_run_task deserializes an array of pipeline invoke entities from file storage and submits each one to a ThreadPoolExecutor with max_workers=10. The number of entities in the array is not bounded by the task itself -- it depends on what was stored in the file. An attacker who controls the file content (via queue poisoning or file storage manipulation) could supply thousands of entities, causing the thread pool to process an unbounded workload. Additionally, each entity spawns a full pipeline execution with database sessions and Flask app contexts, amplifying resource consumption.

In AudioService.transcript_asr(), the entire uploaded file is read into memory via file.read() before the size check (len(file_content) > FILE_SIZE_LIMIT). An attacker can send a file much larger than the 30MB limit, causing the server to allocate that memory before rejecting it. This enables memory exhaustion denial-of-service attacks.

None of the three audio endpoint controllers (console, service_api, web) enforce a Content-Length check or use streaming size-limited reads before passing the file to AudioService. The Flask request.files object will buffer the entire upload. Without a WSGI-level or reverse proxy limit, this allows arbitrarily large payloads to consume server memory.

All three audio controllers access request.files['file'] directly without first checking if the 'file' key exists. If no file is uploaded, this raises a KeyError (HTTP 400) with a raw traceback rather than the intended NoAudioUploadedError. The NoAudioUploadedServiceError check in AudioService only triggers if file is None, but a missing key raises before that.

File type validation in AudioService.transcript_asr() checks file.mimetype against an allowed list of audio MIME types. However, the MIME type is supplied by the client in the Content-Type header of the multipart upload and can be trivially spoofed. No magic-byte or file header validation is performed. An attacker could upload arbitrary content (e.g., a crafted binary) with a spoofed audio/mp3 MIME type, which would pass validation and be forwarded to the speech-to-text model provider.

In AudioService.transcript_asr(), the in-memory buffer is hardcoded with buffer.name = 'temp.mp3' regardless of the actual uploaded file extension or detected MIME type. While this prevents path traversal via filename, it also means that files validated as wav/m4a/amr/mpga are mislabeled as mp3 when passed to the transcription provider, which could cause unexpected behavior or processing errors.

All three audio controllers catch ValueError and re-raise it directly ('raise e'). ValueError exceptions from deeper service layers may contain internal implementation details (e.g., model configuration, workflow state) that get exposed to API consumers in error responses.

The POST /console/api/workspaces/current/members/invite-email endpoint does not check the inviter's role. Any authenticated workspace member can invite new members regardless of their own privilege level. The check_workspace_member_invite_permission() only verifies enterprise workspace-level policy, not the user's role within the workspace.

A user with 'normal' or 'dataset_operator' role can invite new members with the 'admin' role. The only role validation is is_non_owner_role() which permits admin, editor, normal, and dataset_operator. No check compares the inviter's role against the requested invitee role to prevent privilege escalation.

The invitation token generated by RegisterService.invite_new_member() is embedded in a URL and returned in the HTTP response body. This token should only be delivered via the invitation email to limit exposure. Returning it in the response increases the attack surface for token theft.

Generic Exception handler at line 120 returns str(e) directly in the response. Internal error messages may disclose sensitive implementation details such as database schema, file paths, or service internals.

User-supplied email strings from the 'emails' field are passed through with only .lower() normalization. The Pydantic model uses list[str] without EmailStr or regex validation. If downstream services do not validate email format, this could enable email header injection or abuse of the invitation flow for spam.

Hardcoded default PostgreSQL password 'difyai123456' in docker-compose. If .env is not configured, the database is accessible with this well-known credential. An attacker on the same network can connect and exfiltrate all application data.

Hardcoded default SECRET_KEY 'sk-REDACTED' used for Flask session signing and token generation. If unchanged, any attacker can forge session cookies and authentication tokens, achieving full account takeover.

PostgreSQL port 5432 is exposed to the host network by default (EXPOSE_POSTGRES_PORT:-5432). Combined with the hardcoded default password, this allows any host-reachable attacker to directly connect to the database and perform full CRUD operations, data exfiltration, or destructive queries.

The Squid SSRF proxy reverse-proxy section uses 'acl src_all src all' with 'http_access allow src_all', granting unrestricted access to the reverse proxy endpoint. This overrides the earlier 'http_access deny all' for the reverse proxy port, allowing any client that reaches the proxy to access the sandbox backend without authentication.

CORS is configured to allow all origins by default (WEB_API_CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS:-* and CONSOLE_CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS:-*). This permits any website to make authenticated cross-origin requests to the Dify API if a user is logged in, enabling CSRF-like attacks to steal data or perform actions on behalf of the user.

NGINX server_name defaults to '_' (wildcard), accepting requests for any Host header. This can facilitate host-header injection attacks, cache poisoning, and password reset link manipulation if the application uses the Host header to generate URLs.

WEAVIATE_AUTHENTICATION_ANONYMOUS_ACCESS_ENABLED defaults to true in the shared environment block (line 525), though the middleware config sets it to false. If the shared env takes precedence or is used independently, anonymous users can read and write vector data without any authentication.

Plugin daemon binds PLUGIN_REMOTE_INSTALLING_HOST to 0.0.0.0 (line 145), accepting remote plugin installation connections from any interface. Combined with the exposed debugging port and hardcoded SERVER_KEY, an attacker can remotely install arbitrary plugins into the running Dify instance.

The JWT passport issued in the public (non-enterprise) flow has no 'exp' claim in its payload. The payload contains only 'iss', 'sub', 'app_id', 'app_code', and 'end_user_id'. Without expiration, a leaked token grants permanent access. The same issue exists in _exchange_for_public_app_token.

The query parameter 'user_id' is used directly as session_id (line 58). An attacker can craft a URL with a known user_id value. When a victim visits this URL, an EndUser record is created (or looked up) with the attacker-chosen session_id. The attacker can then request a passport with the same user_id to obtain a token bound to the victim's session.

When 'user_id' is provided, the endpoint looks up an existing EndUser by session_id (line 58-60) and issues a JWT passport for that EndUser without any ownership verification. Any unauthenticated caller who knows or guesses a valid session_id can obtain a passport token for another user's session.

In exchange_token_for_existing_web_user, the expiration from the incoming enterprise token (exchanged_token_expires_unix) is used directly as the new token's 'exp' (line 134-135). If the enterprise token has an 'exp' far in the future, the exchanged passport inherits that extended lifetime, ignoring ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRE_MINUTES. The server should enforce its own expiration policy.

The endpoint returns 404 when app_code does not match a valid site and 200 with a token when it does. This differential response allows an attacker to enumerate valid application codes by brute-forcing the X-App-Code header.
